Integrated Electrostimulation Cell Culture Systems Driven by Chemically Modified Twistron Mechanical Energy Harvesting Electrodes

Seongjae Oh, Keon Jung Kim, Chae Hwa Kim, Jun Hyuk Lee, Hyunsoo Kim, Beomsu Kim, Chae-Lin Park, Junho Oh, Eun Sung Kim, Hyun Kim, Sang Young Yeo, Doyong Kim, Xinghao Hu, Joonmyung Choi, Dongseok Suh, Seong Chu Lim, Ray H. Baughman, Chan Hee Park,* Tae Hee Kim,* and Shi Hyeong Kim*

Developing mechanical energy harvesters for electrical stimulation (ES) needed to augment cell behavior is a burgeoning area of interest. Mechanical energy harvesters that can generate electrical energy in electrolyte-containing aqueous environments offer a unique solution for delivering ES to cells. In this work, a fully integrated ES assembly (FESA) is introduced that comprises coiled polydopamine (PDA) containing carbon nanotube yarn (CNT) harvesters, serving as ES generators, and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) coated carbon nanotube (PEDOT/CNT) sheets employed as a conductive scaffold. The PDA containing CNT (PDA/CNT) yarn, a novel twistron electrode, achieves an enhanced electrical power at a lower matching impedance than coiled CNT yarn to efficiently transfer ES to the conductive scaffold. The PEDOT used for the scaffold provides a suitable surface for cell adhesion and low resistance for effective ES transmission. In addition, the upscaled array of coiled PDA/CNT yarns provides an ES current density range up to 75.4  $ \mu $A cm $ ^{-2} $, which is much higher than for ES systems using different mechanical energy harvesters. This FESA is designed to provide an optimal level of ES for the proliferation and differentiation of chondrocytes. The findings illuminate the potential of chemically modified twistron energy harvesters as an innovative and effective strategy to promote biological response.

# Integrated Electrostimulation Cell Culture Systems Driven by Chemically Modified Twistron Mechanical Energy Harvesting Electrodes

Seongjae Oh, Keon Jung Kim, Chae Hwa Kim, Jun Hyuk Lee, Hyunsoo Kim, Beomsu Kim, Chae-Lin Park, Junho Oh, Eun Sung Kim, Hyun Kim, Sang Young Yeo, Doyong Kim, Xinghao Hu, Joonmyung Choi, Dongseok Suh, Seong Chu Lim, Ray H. Baughman, Chan Hee Park,* Tae Hee Kim,* and Shi Hyeong Kim*

Developing mechanical energy harvesters for electrical stimulation (ES) needed to augment cell behavior is a burgeoning area of interest. Mechanical energy harvesters that can generate electrical energy in electrolyte-containing aqueous environments offer a unique solution for delivering ES to cells. In this work, a fully integrated ES assembly (FESA) is introduced that comprises coiled polydopamine (PDA) containing carbon nanotube yarn (CNT) harvesters, serving as ES generators, and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) coated carbon nanotube (PEDOT/CNT) sheets employed as a conductive scaffold. The PDA containing CNT (PDA/CNT) yarn, a novel twistron electrode, achieves an enhanced electrical power at a lower matching impedance than coiled CNT yarn to efficiently transfer ES to the conductive scaffold. The PEDOT used for the scaffold provides a suitable surface for cell adhesion and low resistance for effective ES transmission. In addition, the upscaled array of coiled PDA/CNT yarns provides an ES current density range up to 75.4  $ \mu $A cm $ ^{-2} $, which is much higher than for ES systems using different mechanical energy harvesters. This FESA is designed to provide an optimal level of ES for the proliferation and differentiation of chondrocytes. The findings illuminate the potential of chemically modified twistron energy harvesters as an innovative and effective strategy to promote biological response.

### 1. Introduction

Electrical stimulation (ES) has garnered significant attention with its potential to impact a myriad of biomedical applications, such as neuromodulation and neuroprosthetics, wound healing, cardiac pacing, arrhythmia management, muscle stimulation and rehabilitation, and tissue engineering. $ ^{[1-5]} $ In particular, effective proliferation and differentiation of cells by using ES presents a promising strategy to overcome the challenges associated with regenerative medicine, disease modeling, and drug discovery. $ ^{[6-9]} $

When delivering electricity to target biomaterials, conventional power supplies like batteries, electromagnetic field generators, or direct connections to the electrical grid present several challenges, including limited lifespan, bulky components, and high complexity. $ ^{[10-12]} $ Considering the limitations, energy harvesting technologies are alternative sources of electricity that enable more sustainable, lightweight,

and user-friendly ES systems. Harnessing mechanical energy is especially attractive, since it can convert abundant natural sources (i.e., motion, pressure, flow, or vibration) into electrical energy.

ES systems employing mechanical energy harvesters, such as piezoelectrics and triboelectric nanogenerators, have been recently reported.[13-17] To enhance cell proliferation and differentiation, two important issues remain, which are i) biocompatibility and ii) cell type-specific response. These mechanical energy harvesters have offered the biocompatibility, biodegradability, and minimal material toxicity needed for ES to avoid introducing contaminants or toxic substances that could affect cell behavior. However, electrical current is limited to a narrow range, from a few nanoamperes to several microamperes. Various cell types can require different ranges of ES, necessitating the capability to generate currents tailored for each cell type. For example, currents in the range of 5–15 μA cm⁻² have been reported to promote proliferation and differentiation for chondrocytes,[18,19]

while lower values (0.1–5 μA cm⁻²) may be more suitable for neural stem cells.[16,20,21] In the case of fibroblasts, a slightly wider current range (0.1–17 μA cm⁻²) may be required to stimulate proliferation and differentiation.[22–24] Thus, it is desirable to generate a broad electrical current range capability for ES, so that the modification needs of different specific cell types can be provided.

We here present a novel approach for ES in cell culture systems, called a fully integrated ES assembly (FESA), which exploits twistron mechanical energy harvesters $ ^{[25-28]} $ to deliver a broad ES range. Hydrophilic polydopamine (PDA) was incorporated into the twistron energy harvester electrode to improve performance compared to a guest-free coiled CNT yarn, which magnified the electrical power by a factor of 1.7 (to 1 W kg $ ^{-1} $) and yielded a lower matching impedance (400 Ω) than the coiled CNT yarn, which led to efficient ES generation within the aqueous cell culture medium. To facilitate ES transfer to the cells, a conductive scaffold was prepared using poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythiophene) (PEDOT) coated carbon nanotube sheets, which ensured high electrical conductivity and a suitable surface for cell adhesion. A scale-up strategy, based on application of the ideal circuit model to the twistron energy harvester electrode, provided additional demonstrated improvements of the ES provided by the FESA. As a result, the FESA achieved an ES range of ≈75.4 μA cm $ ^{-2} $, which is outstanding compared to that for ES assemblies based on previous types of mechanical energy harvesters. The behavior observed for meniscal primary cells by applying the FESA system highlights its promising potential for cell culture applications.
2. Results and Discussion
2.1. Design of the FESA

Scheme 1a and Figure S1 (Supporting Information) present a complete FESA designed for enhancing cell proliferation and differentiation. The FESA comprises four primary components: i) a chemically modified (CM)-twistron energy harvester providing the ES, ii) an electrically conducting scaffold receiving ES as an external resistance of the CM-twistron to promote cell growth on their surface, iii) an elastomeric dish that confines the FESA and enables the stretching of the CM-twistrons electrode, and iv) a cell growth medium. Scheme 1b shows the electrolyte immersed CM-twistron energy harvester, including the stretchable CM-twistron electrode and non-deformed Pt mesh counter electrode. The stretchable twistron electrode produces the stretch-induced capacitance changes that generate the electricity. The conductive scaffold (Scheme 1c) is connected between the CM-twistron electrode and the Pt mesh to serve as an external resistance for the CM-twistron harvester. The electrical current delivered from the CM-twistron harvester to the cell-growth-substrate can promote cell proliferation and differentiation as an ES. Detailed information for these two main components (i.e., the CM-twistron harvester and the conductive scaffold) are in Figures 2 and 3, respectively. The elastomeric dish employed for sealing the FESA and simultaneously enabling mechanical deformation of the stretchable twistron electrode is shown in Scheme 1d. The cell culture medium provides an electrolyte environment suitable for both cell growth and the mechanical energy harvesting needed for generating electrical energy (Scheme 1e).

2.2. Fabrication and Harvesting Performances of CPCY

Figure 1a and Figure S2 (Supporting Information) show the fabrication process for the coiled PDA/CNT yarn (CPCY) that is the stretchable energy harvesting electrode in the CM-twistron harvester (Scheme 1b). A vertically-grown CNT forest was synthesized on a silicon wafer by using chemical vapor deposition (Figure S2a(i), Supporting Information). $ ^{[29,30]} $ Two CNT sheet layers were horizontally drawn from a side wall of this forest (Figure S2a(ii), Supporting Information), stacked together so they shared the same CNT alignment direction, and then twist inserted under mechanical load while one sheet-stack end was not torsionally tethered (Figure S2a(iii), Supporting Information). The non-tethering enables preparation of CNT yarn that has low density since it contains little yarn twist (Figure S2a(iv), Supporting Information). $ ^{[26]} $ This low-density CNT yarn was then immersed in a solution of dopamine and tris-HCl buffer to self-polymerize the dopamine while the PDA was inserted into the CNT yarn (Figure 1a). Figure 1b provides spectroscopic evidence that self-polymerization dopamine has been incorporated into the CNT yarn. The peaks at 1615, 1327, and 1028 cm $ ^{-1} $ correspond to catechol aromatic rings, methylene groups, and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. $ ^{[31-33]} $ These functional groups within the PDA confer hydrophilicity to the CNT yarn in the cell growth medium, as shown in Figure 1b inset. The intrinsic contact angle measured on a flat PDA coated surface was 21.1° and 16.3° for advancing and receding angle, respectively. Due to the hydrophilicity of PDA and the porous structure of a CNT yarn, water wicks into the hydrophilic porous matrix resulting in fully wetting surface with 0° of water contact angle. $ ^{[34]} $ Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) images of the cross-section of PDA-incorporated CNT yarn (PDA/CNT yarn) show that the PDA is evenly incorporated within the CNT yarn (Figure 1c). The incorporated PDA remains in the CNT yarn when the much further twist needed to generate a fully coiled CPCY is provided (Figures S2b and S3, Supporting Information).

Figure 1d illustrates mechanical energy harvesting during stretch and release of the twistron harvester that consists of CPCY as a CM-twistron electrode, a Pt mesh as a non-deformed counter electrode, and Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) $ ^{[35]} $ as the aqueous electrolyte (Table S1, Supporting Information). Initially, the CPCY has a bias voltage that is provided by charge donation from the electrolyte, so no voltage source is needed. $ ^{[25,26]} $ To achieve thermodynamic equilibrium, counterions from the electrolyte accumulate at the accessible surface area of the CPCY (Figure 1d–i). $ ^{[36]} $ When the CPCY is stretched, compressive force is applied in the radial direction, causing the distance between the CNTs to decrease (Figure S4, Supporting Information). This densification produces a decrease in capacitance and ion desorption into the electrolyte, causing electrons movement from the counter electrode to the working electrode for electrical compensation (Figure 1d(ii)). Releasing the stress applied to the CPCY enables its return to its initial length, so it recovers its initial density and capacitance, which in turn induces ion reabsorption. Electrons then flow in the opposite direction from the CPCY to the external circuit (Figure 1d).

To clarify the energy harvesting mechanism for the CM-twistron harvester as shown in Figure 1d, it is necessary to verify the physical absorption of ions in CPCY. The physical absorption of ions as a dominant charge storage mechanism for CPCY was investigated from b-value to elucidate the mechanical energy harvesting of CM-twistron. The charge storage mechanism is categorized into capacitive effects and diffusion-controlled effects based on the following equation (i = C₁ ν + C₂ ν¹/² = avᵃ), where i is the current; ν is the sweep rate; C₁ ν and C₂ ν¹/² are the capacitive current and the diffusion-controlled current, respectively.[37,38] Figure 1e shows the b-value as a function of voltage for both coiled CNT yarn used as a conventional twistron electrode and CPCY, which is a twistron electrode modified with PDA. To calculate the b-value, linear sweep voltammetry was carried out in the voltage range of 0.1–0.4 V, encompassing the generated range for both electrodes (Figure 1e and Figure S5, Supporting Information). The b-value falling within the open-circuit voltage (V_OC) ranges of 0.7 ≤ b ≤ 1 and 0.35 ≤ b < 0.7 indicate that the capacitive effect and diffusion-controlled effects as dominant charge storage mechanisms, respectively.[39] In the case of both coiled CNT yarn and CPCY within DMEM, the b-value range from 0.99 and 0.74 at 0.1 V to 0.74 and 0.82

at 0.4 V, respectively. This result indicates that both coiled CNT yarn and CPCY dominantly store charge from the capacitive effect, without involving redox processes between both electrodes and DMEM. Therefore, the charge storage mechanism of CPCY is attributed to the physical adsorption of ions as conventional coiled CNT yarn, and the CM-twistron generates electrical energy from ions absorption/desorption into electrolytes during deformation as shown in Figure 1d.

The harvesting performance of CPCY within DMEM was investigated for the above-mentioned operation mechanism. Since hydrophilic CPCY provides the larger electrochemical accessible area within an aqueous DMEM compared to super-hydrophobic coiled CNT yarn, the CPCY has a 1.3-times higher capacitance of 8.4 F g⁻¹ (Figure 1f). The CPCY provides a decrease in capacitance of 25% under 30% applied tensile strain to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy. Real-time short-circuit-current (I_SC) profiles of the bare coiled CNT yarn and the CPCY were observed under the sinusoidal tensile strain of 30% at 1 Hz (Figure 1g). A 2-times higher peak I_SC of 466.35 A kg⁻¹ was achieved for the CPCY due to its low impedance (Figure S6, Supporting Information). It is noted that the CPCY provides a 180° inverted current phase and opposite V_OC direction compared to the pristine coiled CNT yarn (Figure S5, Supporting Information). The generated I_SC and V_OC direction during sinusoidal strain depends on whether the electrolytes deliver a positive or negative bias voltage into twistron electrodes. This result indicates that both coiled CNT yarn and CPCY dominantly store charge from the capacitive effect, without involving redox processes between both electrodes and DMEM. Therefore, the charge storage mechanism of CPCY is attributed to the physical adsorption of ions as conventional coiled CNT yarn, and the CM-twistron generates electrical energy from ions absorption/desorption into electrolytes during deformation as shown in Figure 1d. Since hydrophilic CPCY provides the larger electrochemical accessible area within an aqueous DMEM compared to super-hydrophobic coiled CNT yarn, the CPCY has a 1.3-times higher capacitance of 8.4 F g⁻¹ (Figure 1f). Figure 1h shows the resistance dependence on gravimetric peak power and peak current. The frequency-dependent peak power of CPCY saturated to 2.1 W kg $ ^{-1} $ for a stretch frequency of 7 Hz and the maximum peak power was maintained at frequencies above 7 Hz (Figure 1i).

The stability for CPCY was investigated by monitoring output performances and comparing morphologies under cyclic test. The CPCY maintains stable  $ I_{SC} $ performance for 2 h, even with higher stretching frequencies including 4 and 7 Hz (Figure S8a, Supporting Information). Moreover, the  $ I_{SC} $ performance of the CPCY remains consistent after one day and shows no signs of degradation across all tested frequencies in a repeating measurement conducted over 7 days (Figure S8b, Supporting Information). Additionally, Figures S3 and S9 (Supporting Information) show stable morphologies and dimensions before and after cyclic test. In Figure S9 (Supporting Information), the EDS mapping images further indicate that the incorporated PDA maintain the stable attachment with CNTs despite of the continuous mechanical deformation. Thus, the CPCY demonstrates stable energy harvesting performances with well-maintained original morphologies and dimensions during the use.

Among the harvesting performance of CPCY, the decrement of matching impedance is a meaningful result in FESA. The low matching impedance (Figure 1h) indicates the enhancement of electricity transfer efficiency when the ES generated from CPCY is provided to the conductive scaffolds. apparent advancing ( $ \theta_{a}^{app} $) and receding contact angle ( $ \theta_{r}^{app} $) were measured with small volume of droplets ( $ V_{drop} = 100 $ nL), as shown in Figure 2c. The contact angle measurement showed that PEDOT coating changed the wettability of the CNT sheets from hydrophobic ( $ \theta_{a,CNT}^{app} = 145.42^\circ $,  $ \theta_{r,CNT}^{app} = 142.51^\circ $) to hydrophilic ( $ \theta_{a,PEDOT/CNT}^{app} = 41.33^\circ $,  $ \theta_{r,PEDOT/CNT}^{app} = 40.42^\circ $). The decrease in contact angles confirmed that the PEDOT was coated on the CNT sheets, since the high surface energy of PEDOT is well known.

In addition, reducing the contact resistance of PEDOT/CNT is crucial for achieving efficient delivery of ES. Figure 2d shows the PEDOT content-dependent sheet resistance ( $ R_s $) measured parallel and perpendicular to CNT sheet alignment. The  $ R_s $ values for CNT sheets measured parallel and perpendicular were 278 and 524  $ \Omega $ sq $ ^{-1} $, respectively, but the CNT alignment dependence of  $ R_s $ diminished with increasing PEDOT wt.\%. These two  $ R_s $ values became similar at 220  $ \Omega $ sq $ ^{-1} $ after coating with PEDOT, and the sheet resistances decreased proportionally with increasing PEDOT wt.\%. In PEDOT/CNT consisting of 39 wt.\% PEDOT, the  $ R_s $ values were low enough, and there were only minor differences between the measuring directions ( $ R_s $ in parallel: 123  $ \Omega $ sq $ ^{-1} $, and  $ R_s $ in vertical: 138  $ \Omega $ sq $ ^{-1} $). The PEDOT/CNT was ultimately optimized as the conductive scaffold due to its enhanced properties, such as suitable  $ S_{dr} $ value, improved hydrophilicity, and low resistance.

The optimized PEDOT/CNT is connected between CPCY and Pt mesh to form the complete assembly. In FESA, the PEDOT/CNT can play two roles, which are as 1) a capacitor that is a part of the counter electrode or as 2) an external resistor passing the current. Figure 2e shows that the PEDOT/CNT has a negligible effect on the Pt mesh counter electrode as a capacitor. Instead, the results indicate the dominant role of the PEDOT/CNT is as a resistor for passing current generated by the CM-twistron harvester in the FESA. The resistance increased from 28 to 100 Ω as the area of the PEDOT/CNT of 39 wt.% PEDOT increased from 5 to 12.5 cm². To assess the ES delivery capability of the scaffolds with different areas, a CM-twistron harvester with 200 μg-weight CPCY provided constant electrical energy while stretching 30% at 1 Hz. As the resistance in the conductive scaffolds increased, peak-to-peak ES (ESpp) of 107.0, 85.7, 74.6, 75.5 μA, and ESpp densities which are normalized to the scaffold area of 21.4, 11.4, 7.5, 6.0 μA cm⁻² were delivered to the conductive scaffolds (Figure 2f). The ESpp density was decreased by increasing the size of the conductive scaffold, due to the PEDOT/CNT acting as a resistor in FESA. For the proliferation and differentiation of a number of cells on large scaffolds, or when cell types require higher ES, it is necessary to solve this reduction of ESpp density. Therefore, it is required to enhance the generated current from the CM-twistron harvesters by upscaling the CPCY.
2.4. Scale-Up Process of CPCY for Enhancing ES

An investigation of the ideal circuit model for CPCY aimed to enhance the  $ E_{Sp} $ density. Figure 3a shows the sequentially deployed circuit model for CPCY, starting from the CNT unit (Figure 3a(i)) and progressing to a CPCY that is scaled-up in both the width (Figure 3a(ii)) and length directions (Figure 3a(iii)). The ideal circuit model for the CNT unit including electrical double-layer capacitor ( $ C_{cl} $), charge transfer resistance ( $ R_{CT} $), and solution resistance ( $ R_{Sol} $) is established when the CNT unit forms the electrical double layer (EDL) within the electrolytes (Figure 3a(i)). $ ^{[41]} $ The cross-section view of a CPCY composed of CNT units within the electrolyte is depicted in Figure 3a(ii). The ideal circuit models are for CNT units arranged in parallel along the interface between CNT units in CPCY and electrolyte. $ ^{[42]} $ Scaling-up in the width direction of CPCY provides more extensive interfaces between CNT units and electrolytes, resulting in the addition of parallel circuitry composed of CNT units (Figure 3a(ii)). Considering the parallel connections in the width direction, the circuit model for width-expanded CPCY is presented as shown in Figure 3a(ii) with elements referred to as  $ C_{width,dl} $,  $ R_{width,ct} $, and  $ R_{width,s} $. Elongating the CPCY leads to the establishment of a parallel network circuit model for width-expanded CPCY, as shown in Figure 3a(iii), due to the enlarged interface between CNT units and electrolytes. Consequently, the total circuit model for scaled-up CPCY is shown in Figure 3a(iv), and this circuit is equivalent to the ideal circuit of the CNT unit (Figure 3a(i)). It is demonstrated through the circuit study of CPCY that the scaling-up in CPCY is a parallel connection of the initial circuit model from the circuit perspective. The parallel connections reduce impedance elements in the CPCY, thereby enhancing generated current. Therefore, scaling-up in both the width and length directions of the CPCY represents an effective strategy for enhancing the  $ E_{Sp} $ density.

Figure 3b shows real-time  $ I_{SC} $ profiles for the pristine CPCY with diameters of 140  $ \mu $m and lengths of 2 cm, width expanded CPCY with diameters of 207  $ \mu $m and lengths of 2 cm (Figure S12, Supporting Information), and elongated CPCY with diameters of 140  $ \mu $m and lengths of 4 cm. The pristine CPCY generates peak  $ I_{SC} $s of 8.2, 22.5, and 54.2  $ \mu $A during stretching to 10%, 20%, and 30%, respectively (Figure 3b). In equivalent tensile conditions, the CPCY with diameters of 207  $ \mu $m and lengths of 2 cm and with diameters of 140  $ \mu $m and lengths of 4 cm generate 32.0, 60.0, and 97.2  $ \mu $A, and 16.2, 50.2, and 97.8  $ \mu $A, respectively. It can be expected that the generated current is proportionally increased as the expanded diameter and the elongated length of pristine CPCY increase. However, the results in Figure 3b provide a discrepancy from the expectation based on the ideal circuit model (Figure 3a). To clarify this difference between the anticipated result from the ideal circuit model and experimental results, the equivalent circuit model for CPCY was investigated from the Nyquist plot (Figure S13a, Supporting Information). In comparison to the ideal circuit model presented in Figure 3a, the fitting model based on the experimental data for CPCY includes the constant phase element (CPE), indicating non-ideal capacitive behavior in porous electrodes (Figure S13b, Supporting Information) $ ^{[43]} $ and a Warburg impedance related to DMEM mass transport. These differences arise due to the presence of an uncontrollable nanoscale porous structure in the CPCY during the fabrication process, thus producing a deviated  $ I_{SC} $ profile for the scaled-up CPCY. Nonetheless, it is noteworthy that the suggested ideal circuit model remains reasonable for explaining the observed increase in generated current within the CPCY.

Frequency-dependent peak-to-peak ESs in FESA are shown in Figure 3d, and Figure S14 (Supporting Information) to compare to other types of mechanical energy harvesters harnessed for the proliferation and differentiation of various kinds of cells (Table S2, Supporting Information). Piezoelectric $ ^{[16,24]} $ and triboelectric nanogenerators $ ^{[14,18-20,22]} $ provide the ES from pico-ampere to several tens of micro-ampere, but they require harsh mechanical frequencies of kilo- to mega-hertz to generate suitable ES. CM-twistron energy harvesters deliver a remarkably large range of  $ ES_{pp} $ density from 3.2  $ \mu $A cm $ ^{-2} $ at 0.5 Hz to 75.4  $ \mu $A cm $ ^{-2} $ at 7 Hz. Compared to other mechanical energy harvesters providing ES for the proliferation and differentiation of cells, the broad range of self-ES in FESA results from three advantages: 1) comparatively high electrical current generated from the CPCY at the low mechanical frequency (<10 Hz), 2) effective enhancement of electrical current from parallel connections in CPCY, and 3) high electrical conductivity and suitable cell adhesion of PEDOT/CNT. These results indicate that the FESA has the potential to provide the required ES for proliferation and differentiation in various cell types.
2.5. Cell Culture Capability of FESA

Prior to investigating the effect of ES from FESA, the cytotoxicity for all components of FESA was evaluated by ISO 10993–5 guidelines. $ ^{[44,45]} $ As shown in Figure 4a,b, the cell viability remains close to 100% of the control even after 48 h of exposure, and no significant differences in cell morphology were observed in all test groups. This suggests that the PEDOT/CNT, CPCY, Pt wire, Pt mesh, and PDMS comprising FESA have suitable biocompatibility for investigating the biological response to ES.

The FESA provides ES to the cells by the stretching/releasing of the energy harvesting units (Video S1, Supporting Information). To demonstrate the valid application capability of FESA for cell culture, the overall setup was prepared in the sterilized incubator. The CM-twistron as an ES provider was two-end-tethered in the elastomer body of FESA, then the integrated system undergoes starching deformation. The assembled CM-twistron was designed to provide suitable ES of 13.1 μA cm⁻² for growth of chondrocytes,[18,19] when the FESA is stretched to 10% at 1 Hz. Furthermore, the conductive scaffold of the PEDOT/CNT sheets was optimized at  $ S_{dr} $ of 2.1 to enhance cell adhesion (Figure S15, Supporting Information). The ES was applied parallel to the alignment direction of the PEDOT/CNT sheets.

As shown in Figure 4c, electrical stimulation is known to elicit augmentation in the synthesis of type I and type II collagen (Col I and Col II) as well as the production of glycosaminoglycans (GAG) across a spectrum of diverse cell populations. $ ^{[46,47]} $ Thus, by promoting the production of Col I, Col II, and GAG, with ES from FESA, it is expected to enhance biological responses, including cell proliferation and differentiation.

Figure 4d–h shows the biological response of meniscal primary cells to ES from FESA, which is after 4 and 7 days of stretching FESA to 10% at 1 Hz to provide ES on the 1 cm x 1 cm PEDOT/CNT sheets (13.1 μA cm⁻²). Figure 4d,e shows the successful proliferation of the primary meniscal cell. Fluorescence microscope images of live/dead cells (Figure 4d) and the quantitative cell counts from colorimetric assays (Figure 4e) revealed a remarkable 7.3-fold promoted cell proliferation in electrically stimulated cells on PEDOT/CNT compared to unstimulated cells at day 7.

As one of the common methods to demonstrate meniscal primary cell differentiation, we quantified key components of the extracellular matrix (ECM), including type I collagen (Col I), type II collagen (Col II), and glycosaminoglycans (GAGs), as shown in Figure 4f–h.[48,49] At day 4, the expression of type I collagen was higher in ES cells, at 228.62 ng μg⁻¹ compared to 67.38 ng μg⁻¹ in unstimulated cells, and on day 7 an even greater amount of Col I was expressed in the cells on FESA (343.59 ng μg⁻¹) (Figure 4f). Col II expression also shows the same trend as Col I expression, with higher expression in ES cells at 54.27 ng μg⁻¹ compared to 22.85 ng μg⁻¹ in unstimulated scaffolds on day 7 (Figure 4g). Significantly higher amounts of Col I and Col II were detected at all time points of culture (days 4 and 7), confirming a rapid high increase in Col I and Col II content. GAG analysis showed that ES cells increased from 4.99 μg μg⁻¹ on day 4 to 6.55 μg μg⁻¹ on day 7, with higher expression compared to unstimulated cells on day 7. (Figure 4h). Variations in the contents of extracellular matrixes in Figure 4f–h verify the effective chondrogenesis of primary meniscal cells from the FESA.[50,51] Consequently, the ES from FESA promoted the proliferation and differentiation of primary meniscal cells. 3. Conclusion

In conclusion, we successfully demonstrated the FESA, which consists of the CM-twistron energy harvester unit including the CPCY and the conductive scaffold as the PEDOT/CNT. Incorporating hydrophilic PDA for CPCY facilitates not only an enhancement of harvesting performance but also the efficient transfer of ES by the lowered matching impedance. To further improve the ES, the electric circuit model study for CPCY provides the most extensive range of  $ ES_{pp} $ density up to 75.4  $ \mu A $ cm $ ^{-2} $. This  $ ES_{pp} $ density range satisfies the required ES for various cell-type, such as chondrocytes, neural stem cells, and fibroblasts. In addition, the PEDOT/CNT conductive scaffold offered hydrophilicity, suitable surface morphology, and high electrical conductivity, which can provide adequate cell adhesion and efficient ES reception. FESA, the newly proposed cell culture system that includes these two optimized components, exhibits promoted proliferation and differentiation compared to the unstimulated cells. We expect the FESA to become a cell-specific customizable ES system through further studies for various cell types.

### 3. Conclusion

In conclusion, we successfully demonstrated the FESA, which consists of the CM-twistron energy harvester unit including the CPCY and the conductive scaffold as the PEDOT/CNT. Incorporating hydrophilic PDA for CPCY facilitates not only an enhancement of harvesting performance but also the efficient transfer of ES by the lowered matching impedance. To further improve the ES, the electric circuit model study for CPCY provides the most extensive range of  $ ES_{pp} $ density up to 75.4  $ \mu A $ cm $ ^{-2} $. This  $ ES_{pp} $ density range satisfies the required ES for various cell-type, such as chondrocytes, neural stem cells, and fibroblasts. In addition, the PEDOT/CNT conductive scaffold offered hydrophilicity, suitable surface morphology, and high electrical conductivity, which can provide adequate cell adhesion and efficient ES reception. FESA, the newly proposed cell culture system that includes these two optimized components, exhibits promoted proliferation and differentiation compared to the unstimulated cells. We expect the FESA to become a cell-specific customizable ES system through further studies for various cell types.
